steps.title: Identify the two independent clauses
steps.content:
The sentence contains two independent clauses: (1) "Kizomba…has become an international [phenomenon]" and (2) "the Indian dance duo…defeated performers…to win the annual Olympiads of Kizomba competition." Two independent clauses must be separated by appropriate punctuation.
  • "phenomenon in": No punctuation between the clauses. This fuses them into a run-on sentence. ✗
  • "phenomenon, in": A comma alone cannot join two independent clauses. This creates a comma splice. ✗
  • "phenomenon and in": "And in" does not grammatically connect an independent clause beginning with "2022, the Indian dance duo…" — the result is ungrammatical. ✗
  • "phenomenon. In": A period correctly ends the first independent clause and begins a new sentence. ✓

steps.title: Confirm the boundary rule
steps.content: Only a period (or semicolon) can separate two independent clauses without a coordinating conjunction. The other three options all fail to properly close the first clause: A has no punctuation (run-on), B has only a comma (comma splice), and C uses "and" in a way that produces an ungrammatical construction. That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
